name: italian grandma soup
notes:
A clean italian soup. Soffrito (yellow onion, celery, carrot) + tomato (both fresh AND paste) as the base, white beans or lentils as the protein, then one of three flavoring directions.

THREE FLAVORING DIRECTIONS: pick one (or layer them lightly):
- old bay + bay leaf
- fennel (powder, bulb, or seeds)
- porcini mushroom powder
Each gives the soup a different identity. All three at once is busy; pairing two is fine.

TOMATO IS NON-OPTIONAL — BOTH FORMS: every time I've left out either fresh tomatoes OR tomato paste, I've been disappointed. The soup ends up flat and unsatisfying. Fresh tomatoes give body and brightness; tomato paste gives concentrated depth and color. Together they make the base sing; alone neither is enough.

NO STOCK / BOUILLON: every time I've made an italian soup from a soffrito and added stock, I've been disappointed — it tastes gummy and artificial. This is a soffrito-and-tomato soup that builds its own body. If you want an injected-stock soup, make something french instead.

HERBS: all fine herbs (parsley, tarragon) clash here — they fight the chosen flavoring axis and muddle the soup. Robust herbs (rosemary, thyme, oregano) are OK as secondary additions for diversity, but unnecessary — the chosen flavoring direction already does most of the flavor work.

GREENS: mixed greens and spinach (flavorless on their own) are excellent additions because they bring bitterness, nutrition, and color without competing with the established flavors.

GLUTAMATE INJECTIONS ARE ANTIPATTERNS: nutritional yeast, soy sauce, dark miso, etc. distract from whichever flavoring backbone you chose. Porcini powder is the one exception (it's already one of the three flavoring directions).

BEANS: white beans are superior to lentils, but both work. Both are better than chickpeas, which are too harsh and break the dish.

POTATOES: optional structural addition. Soaks up the tomato base, gives the soup more chew.
